Based on the newly gathered evidence and reviewing the reasoning provided earlier, I still believe that \boxed{B} Fixed Drug Eruption is the correct diagnosis given the sequence of events (joint pain, fever, rash, hyperpigmentation), localization to the face/nose area post-NSAID administration, and lack of clear signs pointing toward another condition listed among the differentials suggested. Nonetheless, acknowledging further possibilities like rheumatic fever or septic arthritis cannot be completely dismissed pending exclusion criteria being met via follow-up tests as needed - emphasizing a cautious approach ensuring all potential etiologies are ruled out conclusively.
Thus, my final stance stands firm supporting option \( \boxed{\text{B}} \).
This reaffirms our original position while remaining open-minded regarding other less prominent considerations supported robustly now by expanded research insights shared openly here today contributing significantly enhancing our collective understanding amongst professionals engaged deeply into delivering optimal patient care outcomes consistently achieved collaboratively together seamlessly.
